the fraction $\frac{100!}{10^{50}}$ is written as an irreducible fraction, and we are to find its denominator. to do this, we need to analyze the prime factorization of 100! and understand how it relates to the denominator of the fraction when simplified. the denominator of the irreducible fraction will consist of the prime factors of 100! that are not canceled out by the numerator. 

first, we consider the prime factorization of 100!. the factorial of 100, denoted as 100!, is the product of all integers from 1 to 100. the prime factors of 100! include all primes up to 100. to find the denominator of the irreducible fraction, we need to determine which primes are present in the denominator of the fraction $\frac{100!}{10^{50}}$.

the denominator of the fraction is determined by the prime factors of the denominator of the irreducible fraction. the numerator of the fraction is 100!, and the denominator is $10^{50}$. since the fraction is irreducible, any common factors between the numerator and the denominator must have been canceled out. this means that the denominator of the irreducible fraction will contain the prime factors of 100! that are not present in $10^{50}$.

now, we need to examine the prime factors of 100! and $10^{50}$. the prime factorization of 10 is $2 \times 5$. therefore, the prime factorization of $10^{50}$ is $2^{50} \times 5^{50}$. this means that the denominator of the irreducible fraction will include the prime factors of 100! that are not divisible by 2 or 5. 

to find the denominator, we need to calculate the exponents of the prime factors of 100! that are not canceled out by $10^{50}$. this involves calculating the exponent of each prime in 100! and then subtracting the exponent of the same prime in $10^{50}$. the primes that are present in 100! but not in $10^{50}$ will remain in the denominator of the irreducible fraction.
